—These laws appear to have been intended, 1. As a test of the people’s obedience, as Adam was forbidden to eat of the tree of knowledge; and to teach them self-denial and the control of their appetites. 2. To keep the Israelites distinct from other nations. Many of these forbidden animals were also objects of superstition and idolatry to the heathen. 3. The people were taught to make distinctions between the holy and unholy in their companions and close relationships. 4. The law forbade not only the eating of unclean beasts but the touching of them. Those who desire to be kept from any sin must be careful to avoid all temptations to it, or coming near it. The exceptions are very small, and all were designed to call forth constant care and exactness in their obedience; and to teach us to obey. While we enjoy our Christian liberty and are free from such burdensome observances, we must be careful not to abuse our liberty. For the Lord has redeemed and called his people that they may be holy, even as he is holy. We must come out and be separate from the world; we must leave the company of the ungodly and all unnecessary relationships with those who are dead in sin; we must be zealous for good works, devoted followers of God, and companions of his people.
